title: Add HRP optimizer
description:
Add nanobook.optimize_hrp(returns) using hierarchical clustering on correlation + recursive bisection. Robust to ill-conditioned covariance matrices where Markowitz blows up.

**Technical Specification:**
optimize_hrp(returns: &[Vec<f64>]) -> OptimizerResult
- Compute correlation matrix from returns
- Convert to distance matrix: distance = sqrt(0.5 * (1 - correlation))
- Hierarchical clustering (single linkage or average linkage)
- Recursive bisection: split dendrogram into clusters, assign inverse variance weights
- Return OptimizerResult with weights, iters, converged, final_step_squared

Clustering options:
- Linkage method: Single, Average, Complete (configurable)
- Distance metric: 1 - correlation (standard for HRP)
- Cluster allocation: inverse variance weighting within each cluster

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure numerical optimization, no domain-specific assumptions
- Works with any returns matrix (not just equities)
- Generic portfolio construction applicable to any asset class
- No qtrade-specific data structures or assumptions

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property tests: weights sum to ~1, all non-negative, cluster structure valid
- Property test: handles degenerate correlation matrices (perfect correlation)
- Reference parity: compare vs sklearn hierarchical clustering + López de Prado reference
- Edge cases: single asset, perfectly correlated assets, degenerate correlation matrix
- Numerical stability: handle ill-conditioned covariance matrices

**Implementation Guidance:**
- Extend optimize.rs with new optimize_hrp function
- May need hierarchical clustering implementation or dependency
- Consider scientific computing dependency (ndarray/linfa) or implement from scratch
- Start with simple linkage method (single) before complex methods
- Follow existing OptimizerResult patterns
- Python binding: py_optimize_hrp
- Add docstring with López de Prado (2016) reference

**Files:** src/optimize.rs (extend), python/src/lib.rs
**Dependencies:** May need scientific computing library for clustering (evaluate options)
close_reason: Successfully implemented. Added correlation_matrix, correlation_to_distance, single_linkage_clustering, recursive_bisection, optimize_hrp functions to src/optimize.rs (452 lines). No external dependencies, clustering from scratch. 14 new tests, all 283 tests pass. Python bindings added. Follows OptimizerResult patterns, López de Prado (2016) reference.

title: Add tear sheet result struct
description:
Add nanobook.TearSheet (or extend BacktestResult) with full reporting payload: monthly returns matrix, rolling Sharpe series, drawdown events, trade-level analytics. Data layer only, rendering belongs in consumers.

**Technical Specification:**
TearSheet struct (or extend BacktestResult):
- monthly_returns: Matrix<f64> (months × years or similar structure)
- rolling_sharpe: Vec<f64> (e.g., 90-day rolling Sharpe)
- drawdown_events: Vec<DrawdownEvent> (from drawdown analytics)
- trade_analytics: TradeAnalytics { entry_exit_distribution, hold_time_histogram, win_loss_size_distribution }
- aggregate_metrics: Metrics (existing performance metrics)

TradeAnalytics details:
- entry_exit_distribution: Map<ExitReason, usize> (count trades by exit reason)
- hold_time_histogram: Vec<usize> (distribution of trade durations)
- win_loss_size_distribution: {win_sizes: Vec<f64>, loss_sizes: Vec<f64>} (P&L distribution)

**Generalization Check:**
- Data aggregation only, no rendering logic
- Works with any backtest result (not just qtrade strategies)
- Generic reporting structure applicable to any trading system
- No qtrade-specific visualization or formatting assumptions

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property tests: monthly returns sum to total return, trade counts match
- Property test: rolling Sharpe computed correctly over windows
- Edge cases: single month, no trades, constant returns, short backtest
- Serializability: struct can be serialized to JSON/Parquet for consumer rendering

**Implementation Guidance:**
- Depends on bd-7vj (drawdown analytics) for DrawdownEvent
- Consider extending BacktestResult rather than new TearSheet struct
- Monthly returns: group returns by month/year, compute monthly P&L
- Rolling Sharpe: sliding window computation using existing Metrics
- Trade analytics: need trade-level data from backtest (may require BacktestResult extension)
- Python binding: py_tear_sheet or auto-populated field in BacktestResult
- Keep data-only — no HTML/plotting logic (consumer's responsibility)

**Files:** src/result.rs (extend BacktestResult) or new report.rs, src/portfolio/metrics.rs (for trade analytics), python/src/lib.rs
**Dependency:** bd-7vj (drawdown analytics)

Extended Python bindings (PyBacktestResult). 8 new tests, all tests pass. Data-only implementation, trade analytics and drawdown events deferred as specified. No breaking changes.

title: Phase 3.1: Tracing Infrastructure - Dependencies and subscriber setup
description:
## Background and Context

Phase 3 (Observability) should wait for Phase 1+2 to complete since structured logging helps debug both features. We need to add tracing infrastructure to replace the existing log crate with structured logging.

## Objective

Add tracing dependencies to rebalancer/Cargo.toml (tracing, tracing-subscriber, tracing-appender, tracing-log). Replace env_logger with tracing-subscriber in main.rs. Configure JSON formatter for stdout and file appender for audit log separation. Add correlation ID generation for each rebalance run.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Tracing Dependencies

Add to rebalancer/Cargo.toml:
- tracing (core tracing API)
- tracing-subscriber (subscriber implementations)
- tracing-appender (file logging)
- tracing-log (compatibility with existing log crate)

### Replace env_logger with tracing-subscriber

In main.rs:
- Remove env_logger::init()
- Add tracing_subscriber::fmt() with JSON formatter
- Configure subscriber with layers (JSON formatter, file appender)
- Set up global default subscriber

### Configure JSON Formatter

- Use tracing-subscriber::fmt::format().json() for JSON output
- Configure pretty printing for development (optional)
- Ensure structured fields are consistent

### Add File Appender

- Use tracing-appender for file-based logging
- Separate audit log from general logs
- Configure log rotation if needed

### Add Correlation ID Generation

- Generate unique correlation ID for each rebalance run
- Use UUID v4 or timestamp-based ID
- Include correlation ID in all log entries via span context

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test tracing setup:
- test_tracing_dependencies_compiled()
- test_subscriber_initialized()
- test_json_formatter_configured()
- test_file_appender_configured()
- test_correlation_id_generation()
- test_correlation_id_unique()

Test compatibility:
- test_tracing_log_compatibility()
- test_existing_log_calls_work()

### Integration Tests

Test log output:
- test_log_output_valid_json()
- test_log_output_includes_span_context()
- test_log_output_includes_correlation_id()
- test_file_appender_writes_logs()
- test_stdout_formatter_outputs_json()

Test correlation ID:
- test_correlation_id_propagated_to_spans()
- test_correlation_id_in_all_log_entries()
- test_correlation_id_unique_per_run()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/tracing_output.json: Sample JSON log output
- fixtures/tracing_with_correlation.json: Log with correlation ID

Test: test_golden_fixture_tracing_output(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- File appender write failure
- Disk full during logging
- Invalid JSON output
- Correlation ID collision
- Subscriber initialization failure
- Multiple runs with same correlation ID
- Log rotation scenarios

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Tracing Dependencies Fail to Compile

**Detection:**
- Cargo build fails
- Dependency conflicts
- Version incompatibilities

**Impact:**
- Cannot add tracing infrastructure
- Development blocked
- Must use existing log crate

**Recovery:**
- Fix dependency versions
- Resolve conflicts
- Use compatible versions
- Test build with tracing dependencies

### If Subscriber Initialization Fails

**Detection:**
- Panic on startup
- Subscriber not initialized
- Logs not output

**Impact:**
- Application cannot start
- No logging capability
- Debugging impossible

**Recovery:**
- Fix subscriber configuration
- Add fallback to env_logger
- Test subscriber initialization
- Review initialization code

### If JSON Formatter Fails

**Detection:**
- Invalid JSON output
- Formatter panics
- Structured fields missing

**Impact:**
- Logs not parseable
- Log aggregation broken
- Debugging difficult

**Recovery:**
- Fix JSON formatter configuration
- Add validation for JSON output
- Test formatter with various log levels
- Review formatter settings

### If File Appender Fails

**Detection:**
- File write errors
- Disk full
- Permission denied

**Impact:**
- Logs not written to file
- Log history lost
- Audit trail incomplete

**Recovery:**
- Retry file writes with exponential backoff
- Fall back to stdout only
- Check disk space and permissions
- Review file appender configuration

### If Correlation ID Generation Fails

**Detection:**
- UUID generation fails
- Collision detected
- Correlation ID missing from logs

**Impact:**
- Cannot trace requests across logs
- Debugging difficult
- Log aggregation less useful

**Recovery:**
- Fix correlation ID generation
- Use fallback timestamp-based ID
- Add collision detection
- Review generation logic

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Tracing overhead: ~10-50μs per log call
- JSON serialization overhead: ~5-20μs per log
- File appender overhead: ~1-5ms per log write
- Total overhead: < 5% of application time

**Measurement:**
- Benchmark log call latency with tracing
- Measure JSON serialization overhead
- Measure file appender write latency
- Profile overall application performance

**Thresholds:**
- Log call latency: < 100μs
- JSON serialization: < 50μs
- File appender write: < 10ms
- Overall performance impact: < 5%

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable tracing if:
- Performance degradation > 10%
- JSON output invalid
- File appender fails > 5% of writes
- Correlation ID missing from logs > 1%

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- Log output rate (should match expected volume)
- JSON parse success rate (should be 100%)
- File appender write success rate (should be > 99%)
- Correlation ID presence rate (should be 100%)
- Log latency (p95 < 10ms)

### Incident Response

If tracing issues detected:
1. Fall back to env_logger if possible
2. Check disk space and permissions
3. Verify JSON formatter configuration
4. Review correlation ID generation
5. Fix underlying issue
6. Re-enable after fix verified in staging

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Tracing doesn't directly interact with broker
- Logs broker interactions with span context
- Correlation ID helps trace broker calls

**Audit Log Integration:**
- Tracing separates general logs from audit log
- Audit log remains unchanged
- Tracing logs complement audit log

**Feature Flag Integration:**
- Tracing can be enabled independently
- No feature flag needed for tracing
- Can be toggled via configuration

## Why This Matters

Structured logging with tracing provides better observability and debugging capability. JSON output enables log aggregation and analysis. Correlation IDs help trace requests across distributed systems. Span context provides hierarchical debugging information.

## Considerations

- Tracing adds overhead, must monitor performance
- JSON output increases log size
- File appender needs disk space management
- Correlation ID must be unique per run
- Backward compatibility with existing log calls
- Migration from log crate to tracing should be gradual

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Tracing dependencies added
- [ ] env_logger replaced with tracing-subscriber
- [ ] JSON formatter configured
- [ ] File appender set up
- [ ] Correlation ID generation implemented
- [ ] Logs output valid JSON
- [ ] Unit tests verify tracing setup
- [ ] Integration tests verify log output
- [ ] Golden fixture tests pass
- [ ] Edge case tests cover failure scenarios
- [ ] Performance thresholds met
- [ ] Rollback criteria documented
- [ ] Monitoring requirements specified
- [ ] Incident response procedures documented

title: Phase 2.2: Two-Phase Kill Workflow - Graceful then forceful
description:
## Background and Context

Graceful shutdown may not work if the process is stuck or unresponsive. We need a two-phase kill workflow: try graceful shutdown first, then forceful cancellation if it times out.

## Objective

Implement two-phase kill workflow in the kill command. Phase 1: send SIGTERM, wait for graceful shutdown (30s timeout). Phase 2: if timeout, connect to broker directly and cancel orders.

## Detailed Implementation

### Phase 1: Graceful Shutdown

- Send SIGTERM to rebalancer process
- Wait for graceful shutdown (30s timeout)
- Write KillPhase1Started and KillPhase1Completed audit events
- Monitor process exit

### Phase 2: Forceful Cancellation

If Phase 1 times out:
- Write KillPhase2Started audit event
- Connect to broker directly (bypass the stuck process)
- Cancel all open orders at broker
- Write KillPhase2Completed audit event

### Audit Events

Add kill phase audit events:
- KillPhase1Started
- KillPhase1Completed
- KillPhase2Started
- KillPhase2Completed

### Timeout Configuration

Make timeout configurable (default 30s):
- Config option in config.toml
- Command-line flag override
- Environment variable support

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test two-phase workflow:
- test_phase1_graceful_shutdown_succeeds()
- test_phase1_graceful_shutdown_times_out()
- test_phase2_forceful_cancellation_triggered()
- test_phase2_skipped_if_phase1_succeeds()

Test timeout configuration:
- test_timeout_from_config()
- test_timeout_from_command_line()
- test_timeout_from_environment()
- test_default_timeout_used()

Test audit events:
- test_kill_phase1_started_event()
- test_kill_phase1_completed_event()
- test_kill_phase2_started_event()
- test_kill_phase2_completed_event()

### Integration Tests

Test two-phase kill:
- test_two_phase_kill_graceful_succeeds()
- test_two_phase_kill_graceful_times_out()
- test_two_phase_kill_forceful_cancels_orders()
- test_two_phase_kill_audit_log_complete()

Test timeout scenarios:
- test_timeout_10s()
- test_timeout_30s()
- test_timeout_60s()
- test_timeout_custom()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/phase1_success.jsonl: Graceful shutdown succeeds
- fixtures/phase1_timeout_phase2_success.jsonl: Phase 1 times out, Phase 2 succeeds
- fixtures/phase2_partial_failure.jsonl: Phase 2 partially fails

Test: test_golden_fixture_phase1_success(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- Process exits during Phase 1
- Process crashes during Phase 1
- Broker connection fails during Phase 2
- Cancellation fails during Phase 2
- Timeout value is zero or negative
- Multiple kill commands sent
- Audit log write fails during either phase
- Process already dead when kill command sent

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Phase 1 Fails to Send SIGTERM

**Detection:**
- SIGTERM send fails
- Process not found
- Permission denied

**Impact:**
- Cannot attempt graceful shutdown
- Must proceed to Phase 2 immediately
- Forceful cancellation required

**Recovery:**
- Proceed to Phase 2 (forceful cancellation)
- Check process ID is correct
- Verify permissions to send signal
- Review signal sending logic

### If Phase 1 Times Out

**Detection:**
- Process doesn't exit within timeout
- Process still running after timeout
- Phase 1 completed event not written

**Impact:**
- Graceful shutdown failed
- Process is stuck or unresponsive
- Forceful cancellation required

**Recovery:**
- Proceed to Phase 2 (forceful cancellation)
- Cancel orders directly at broker
- Investigate why process is stuck
- Review graceful shutdown implementation

### If Phase 2 Broker Connection Fails

**Detection:**
- Cannot connect to broker
- Broker API unavailable
- Network failure

**Impact:**
- Cannot cancel orders directly
- Orders remain at broker
- Must cancel manually

**Recovery:**
- Retry broker connection with exponential backoff
- Log error with remaining order IDs
- Cancel orders manually via broker UI
- Review broker connection logic

### If Phase 2 Cancellation Fails

**Detection:**
- broker.cancel_order() returns error
- Orders not cancelled
- Cancellation timeout

**Impact:**
- Orders remain at broker
- Potential for unwanted fills
- Must cancel manually

**Recovery:**
- Retry cancellation with exponential backoff
- Log error with remaining order IDs
- Cancel orders manually via broker UI
- Review cancellation logic

### If Audit Log Write Fails

**Detection:**
- Audit log write returns error
- Kill phase events not written
- Audit log incomplete

**Impact:**
- Audit trail incomplete
- Cannot track which phase was used
- Operational visibility reduced

**Recovery:**
- Log error to stderr
- Continue with kill workflow
- Fix audit log issue after restart
- Review audit log write logic

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Phase 1: up to 30s wait for graceful shutdown
- Phase 2: broker connection + cancellation (~5-10s)
- Total kill time: up to 40s
- No impact on normal operation

**Measurement:**
- Measure Phase 1 duration (should be < 30s)
- Measure Phase 2 duration (should be < 10s)
- Measure total kill time (should be < 40s)
- Profile broker connection overhead

**Thresholds:**
- Phase 1 timeout: 30s (configurable)
- Phase 2 duration: < 10s
- Total kill time: < 40s
- Broker connection latency: < 5s

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable two-phase kill if:
- Phase 1 fails to send SIGTERM consistently
- Phase 2 broker connection fails > 20%
- Cancellation failures > 10%
- Total kill time > 60s

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- Kill command success rate (should be > 99%)
- Phase 1 success rate (should be > 90%)
- Phase 2 success rate (should be > 95%)
- Phase 1 duration (p95 < 30s)
- Phase 2 duration (p95 < 10s)
- Total kill time (p95 < 40s)
- Audit log write errors during kill (should be zero)

### Incident Response

If two-phase kill issues detected:
1. Check process status (is it actually stuck?)
2. Verify broker connectivity
3. Cancel orders manually via broker UI if needed
4. Investigate root cause (logs, metrics)
5. Fix signal sending or broker connection logic
6. Re-enable after fix verified in staging

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Phase 2 connects to broker directly
- Uses broker.open_orders() and broker.cancel_order()
- Broker must support cancellation
- Broker connection must work independently of rebalancer process

**Audit Log Integration:**
- Writes KillPhase1Started, KillPhase1Completed
- Writes KillPhase2Started, KillPhase2Completed
- Audit log must be accessible to kill command
- Audit log writes should not block kill workflow

**Process Integration:**
- Phase 1 sends SIGTERM to rebalancer process
- Monitors process exit
- Phase 2 bypasses process entirely
- Kill command must have correct process ID

## Why This Matters

Two-phase kill provides a safety net for when graceful shutdown fails. If the process is stuck or unresponsive, Phase 2 ensures orders are still cancelled at the broker. This is critical for operational safety and preventing unwanted fills.

## Considerations

- Timeout should be configurable for different environments
- Phase 2 must work independently of the stuck process
- Audit log writes should not block kill workflow
- Multiple kill commands should be handled safely
- Process may exit during Phase 1, handle gracefully

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Two-phase workflow implemented
- [ ] Graceful shutdown attempted first with timeout
- [ ] Forceful cancellation if graceful times out
- [ ] Audit log captures both phases
- [ ] Integration tests verify both phases work
- [ ] Timeout configuration implemented
- [ ] Unit tests verify workflow and configuration
- [ ] Golden fixture tests pass
- [ ] Edge case tests cover failure scenarios
- [ ] Performance thresholds met
- [ ] Rollback criteria documented
- [ ] Monitoring requirements specified
- [ ] Incident response procedures documented

title: Phase 1.6B: Phased Rollout - Extend to positions and quotes
description:
## Background and Context

After validating write-ahead logging for orders (Phase 1A), we extend it to positions and quotes (Phase 1B). These are read-only operations but still need write-ahead logging for complete crash recovery coverage.

## Objective

Extend write-ahead logging to broker.positions() and broker.quotes() calls. Add intent logging before these calls and result logging after. Test and validate the extended coverage.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Intent Logging for Positions

Add PositionsIntent checkpoint and log_positions_intent() function:

- Log intent before calling broker.positions()
- Log result after positions are fetched
- Recovery can detect incomplete position fetches
- Metadata: timestamp, target spec reference

### Add Intent Logging for Quotes

Add QuotesIntent checkpoint and log_quotes_intent() function:

- Log intent before calling broker.quotes()
- Log result after quotes are fetched
- Recovery can detect incomplete quote fetches
- Metadata: symbols requested, staleness threshold

### Update Recovery Logic

Extend recovery to handle PositionsIntent and QuotesIntent:

- Detect incomplete intents (intent without result)
- Query broker to reconcile if needed
- Update audit log with reconciliation results

### Testing

- Test position fetch write-ahead in dry-run mode
- Test quote fetch write-ahead in dry-run mode
- Verify recovery handles incomplete fetches
- Performance testing for additional logging overhead

### Validation

- Dry-run testing passes
- Staging testing validates broker reconciliation
- Production rollout with monitoring
- Performance degradation < 10%

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test position intent logging:
- test_log_positions_intent_writes_valid_json()
- test_log_positions_intent_includes_metadata()
- test_log_positions_result_logs_correctly()

Test quote intent logging:
- test_log_quotes_intent_writes_valid_json()
- test_log_quotes_intent_includes_metadata()
- test_log_quotes_result_logs_correctly()

Test recovery for positions/quotes:
- test_recovery_handles_incomplete_positions_intent()
- test_recovery_handles_incomplete_quotes_intent()
- test_recovery_reconciles_positions_with_broker()
- test_recovery_reconciles_quotes_with_broker()

### Integration Tests

Test position fetch write-ahead:
- test_positions_fetch_with_write_ahead()
- test_positions_fetch_crash_recovery()
- test_positions_fetch_audit_log_valid()

Test quote fetch write-ahead:
- test_quotes_fetch_with_write_ahead()
- test_quotes_fetch_crash_recovery()
- test_quotes_fetch_audit_log_valid()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/positions_intent_only.jsonl: PositionsIntent without result
- fixtures/positions_intent_success.jsonl: PositionsIntent + result
- fixtures/quotes_intent_only.jsonl: QuotesIntent without result
- fixtures/quotes_intent_success.jsonl: QuotesIntent + result

Test: test_golden_fixture_positions_intent(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- Crash during positions fetch
- Crash during quotes fetch
- Broker query fails during reconciliation
- Positions fetch returns stale data
- Quotes fetch returns stale data
- Multiple symbols in quotes request
- Empty positions or quotes
- Audit log write fails after intent

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Position Intent Logging Fails

**Detection:**
- log_positions_intent() returns error
- Positions fetch cannot proceed
- Audit log write error

**Impact:**
- Positions fetch fails
- Rebalancer cannot get current positions
- May need to retry or abort

**Recovery:**
- Retry positions fetch after audit log fixed
- Check disk space and permissions
- If persistent, may need to disable write-ahead for positions

### If Quote Intent Logging Fails

**Detection:**
- log_quotes_intent() returns error
- Quotes fetch cannot proceed
- Audit log write error

**Impact:**
- Quotes fetch fails
- Rebalancer cannot get current quotes
- May need to retry or abort

**Recovery:**
- Retry quotes fetch after audit log fixed
- Check disk space and permissions
- If persistent, may need to disable write-ahead for quotes

### If Broker Reconciliation Fails for Positions

**Detection:**
- Broker query fails during recovery
- Cannot determine positions state
- Recovery action set to MANUAL_REVIEW

**Impact:**
- Recovery requires manual review
- Potential delay in resuming operations
- May need to manually fetch positions

**Recovery:**
- Operator manually fetches positions from broker
- Operator updates audit log with findings
- Recovery proceeds after manual resolution

### If Broker Reconciliation Fails for Quotes

**Detection:**
- Broker query fails during recovery
- Cannot determine quotes state
- Recovery action set to MANUAL_REVIEW

**Impact:**
- Recovery requires manual review
- Potential delay in resuming operations
- May need to manually fetch quotes

**Recovery:**
- Operator manually fetches quotes from broker
- Operator updates audit log with findings
- Recovery proceeds after manual resolution

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Additional audit log writes for positions and quotes
- Positions fetch: ~1-2ms overhead
- Quotes fetch: ~1-2ms overhead
- Slightly increased audit log size

**Measurement:**
- Benchmark positions fetch latency with and without write-ahead
- Benchmark quotes fetch latency with and without write-ahead
- Measure audit log size growth rate
- Profile overall rebalancer performance

**Thresholds:**
- Positions fetch latency increase < 10%
- Quotes fetch latency increase < 10%
- Audit log size growth < 5% per run
- Overall rebalancer performance degradation < 5%

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable write_ahead_logging feature flag if:
- Positions or quotes fetch failures increase > 5%
- Performance degradation > 15%
- Audit log corruption for positions/quotes
- Recovery failures for positions/quotes
- Any unexpected behavior

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- Positions fetch success rate (should be > 99%)
- Quotes fetch success rate (should be > 99%)
- Positions fetch latency (p95 < 5s baseline)
- Quotes fetch latency (p95 < 2s baseline)
- Audit log write error rate (should be zero)
- Recovery success rate for positions/quotes (should be 100%)

### Incident Response

If issues detected:
1. Disable write_ahead_logging feature flag
2. Verify positions and quotes fetch return to normal
3. Investigate root cause (audit logs, metrics)
4. Fix underlying issue
5. Re-enable after fix verified in staging

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Positions fetch uses write-ahead wrapper
- Quotes fetch uses write-ahead wrapper
- Broker reconciliation queries broker for positions/quotes state

**Audit Log Integration:**
- PositionsIntent and PositionsResult checkpoints
- QuotesIntent and QuotesResult checkpoints
- Audit log parsing handles new checkpoint types
- Recovery system parses new checkpoint types

**Feature Flag Integration:**
- write_ahead_logging feature flag gates positions/quotes logic
- When disabled, positions/quotes use direct broker calls
- Feature flag toggle requires rebuild and redeploy

## Why This Matters

Extending write-ahead logging to positions and quotes completes the coverage for read-only operations. While these don't directly submit orders, they're critical for rebalancing decisions. Crash recovery needs to know if positions or quotes were successfully fetched to avoid making decisions based on stale or incomplete data.

## Considerations

- Positions and quotes are read-only, so write-ahead is for detection only
- Recovery may not need to reconcile (can just refetch)
- Staleness thresholds for quotes are important
- Positions are typically small, quotes can be large (many symbols)
- Performance impact is minimal for these operations

## Success Criteria

- [ ] PositionsIntent and QuotesIntent checkpoints added
- [ ] Intent logging functions implemented
- [ ] Recovery handles incomplete position/quote fetches
- [ ] Dry-run testing passes
- [ ] Staging testing validates broker reconciliation
- [ ] Production rollout successful
- [ ] Performance degradation < 10%
- [ ] Unit tests verify intent logging
- [ ] Integration tests verify write-ahead behavior
- [ ] Golden fixture tests pass
- [ ] Edge case tests cover failure scenarios
- [ ] Rollback criteria documented
- [ ] Monitoring requirements specified
- [ ] Incident response procedures documented

title: Phase 2.4: Kill Switch Audit Events - KillRequested and KillCompleted
description:
## Background and Context

The kill switch operations need audit trail entries for accountability and debugging. We need KillRequested and KillCompleted events that capture the full kill sequence.

## Objective

Add KillRequested and KillCompleted audit events with metadata. KillRequested captures trigger time and method. KillCompleted captures summary: method, orders_cancelled_count, orders_remaining_count, duration_seconds, error messages.

## Detailed Implementation

### KillRequested Event

Add KillRequested checkpoint to audit log:
- Trigger time (timestamp)
- Method (graceful/forceful)
- Trigger source (command, signal, etc.)

### KillCompleted Event

Add KillCompleted checkpoint with summary:
- method (graceful/forced)
- orders_cancelled_count
- orders_remaining_count (if verification failed)
- duration_seconds
- error_messages (if any)

### Update Kill Command

Modify kill command to write audit events:
- Write KillRequested when kill is triggered
- Write KillCompleted when kill sequence finishes
- Handle both graceful and forceful paths

### Update Recovery

Update recovery to parse kill events:
- Reconstruct kill sequence from audit log
- Understand which method was used
- Know if kill completed successfully

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test KillRequested event:
- test_kill_requested_event_serializes_correctly()
- test_kill_requested_includes_timestamp()
- test_kill_requested_includes_method()
- test_kill_requested_includes_trigger_source()

Test KillCompleted event:
- test_kill_completed_event_serializes_correctly()
- test_kill_completed_includes_method()
- test_kill_completed_includes_orders_cancelled()
- test_kill_completed_includes_orders_remaining()
- test_kill_completed_includes_duration()
- test_kill_completed_includes_error_messages()

Test kill command integration:
- test_kill_command_writes_kill_requested()
- test_kill_command_writes_kill_completed()
- test_kill_command_handles_graceful_path()
- test_kill_command_handles_forceful_path()

Test recovery parsing:
- test_recovery_parses_kill_requested()
- test_recovery_parses_kill_completed()
- test_recovery_reconstructs_kill_sequence()
- test_recovery_understands_kill_method()

### Integration Tests

Test audit trail:
- test_kill_audit_trail_complete()
- test_kill_audit_trail_graceful()
- test_kill_audit_trail_forceful()
- test_kill_audit_trail_with_errors()

Test recovery with kill events:
- test_recovery_with_kill_sequence()
- test_recovery_determines_kill_method()
- test_recovery_knows_kill_success()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/kill_requested.jsonl: KillRequested event
- fixtures/kill_completed_graceful.jsonl: KillCompleted graceful
- fixtures/kill_completed_forced.jsonl: KillCompleted forced
- fixtures/kill_with_errors.jsonl: Kill with error messages

Test: test_golden_fixture_kill_requested(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- Audit log write fails for KillRequested
- Audit log write fails for KillCompleted
- Kill sequence interrupted (process crash)
- KillCompleted with zero orders cancelled
- KillCompleted with remaining orders
- KillCompleted with multiple error messages
- KillRequested without KillCompleted (incomplete)
- Multiple kill sequences in audit log

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If KillRequested Write Fails

**Detection:**
- Audit log write returns error
- KillRequested event not written
- Error logged to stderr

**Impact:**
- Audit trail incomplete
- Cannot track when kill was triggered
- Operational visibility reduced

**Recovery:**
- Log error to stderr
- Continue with kill sequence (audit log not critical)
- Fix audit log issue after restart
- Review audit log write logic

### If KillCompleted Write Fails

**Detection:**
- Audit log write returns error
- KillCompleted event not written
- Error logged to stderr

**Impact:**
- Audit trail incomplete
- Cannot track kill result
- Cannot verify kill success from audit log
- Operational visibility reduced

**Recovery:**
- Log error to stderr
- Continue with shutdown (audit log not critical)
- Fix audit log issue after restart
- Review audit log write logic

### If Kill Sequence Interrupted

**Detection:**
- KillRequested written but no KillCompleted
- Process crash during kill sequence
- Audit log incomplete

**Impact:**
- Audit trail incomplete
- Recovery cannot determine kill result
- Unknown state of orders

**Recovery:**
- Recovery must query broker for order status
- Recovery must infer kill was attempted
- Operator may need manual intervention
- Review kill sequence robustness

### If Metadata Missing

**Detection:**
- Kill event missing required fields
- Serialization fails
- Parsing fails

**Impact:**
- Audit event invalid
- Cannot parse audit log
- Recovery may fail

**Recovery:**
- Fix serialization logic
- Ensure all required fields are present
- Add validation before writing
- Review event structure

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Two additional audit log writes per kill
- Audit log write latency: ~1-2ms per event
- Total overhead: ~2-4ms per kill
- No impact on kill sequence timing

**Measurement:**
- Measure audit log write latency for kill events
- Measure total kill time with audit logging
- Profile audit log size impact

**Thresholds:**
- Audit log write latency: < 5ms per event
- Total kill time increase: < 10ms
- Audit log size increase: < 1KB per kill

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable kill audit events if:
- Audit log write failures > 10%
- Audit log corruption due to kill events
- Performance degradation > 5%
- Parsing failures for kill events

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- KillRequested event write rate
- KillCompleted event write rate
- Audit log write errors for kill events (should be zero)
- Kill event parsing success rate (should be 100%)
- Time between KillRequested and KillCompleted

### Incident Response

If audit event issues detected:
1. Continue with kill sequence (audit log not critical)
2. Log errors to stderr
3. Investigate root cause (disk space, permissions)
4. Fix audit log issue after restart
5. Re-enable after fix verified in staging

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Kill events don't directly interact with broker
- Events capture broker interaction results
- Broker state may be queried for verification

**Audit Log Integration:**
- Writes KillRequested and KillCompleted events
- Events are part of checkpoint enum
- Recovery parses these events
- Audit log must be accessible to kill command

**Kill Workflow Integration:**
- KillRequested written at start of kill sequence
- KillCompleted written at end of kill sequence
- Both graceful and forceful paths write events
- Events capture full kill sequence

## Why This Matters

Audit events for the kill switch provide accountability and debugging capability. Knowing when a kill was triggered, which method was used, and the result is critical for operational safety and post-incident analysis.

## Considerations

- Audit log write failures should not block kill sequence
- Events must include sufficient metadata for debugging
- Recovery must handle incomplete kill sequences
- Events should be parsable by external tools
- Multiple kill sequences should be distinguishable

## Success Criteria

- [ ] KillRequested and KillCompleted checkpoints added
- [ ] Kill command writes audit events correctly
- [ ] Recovery parses kill events successfully
- [ ] Integration tests verify audit trail
- [ ] Unit tests verify event serialization
- [ ] Golden fixture tests pass
- [ ] Edge case tests cover failure scenarios
- [ ] Performance thresholds met
- [ ] Rollback criteria documented
- [ ] Monitoring requirements specified
- [ ] Incident response procedures documented

title: Add walk-forward analysis primitive
description:
Add nanobook.walkforward(returns, params, n_windows, train_pct) for rolling re-fit with IS/OOS metrics per window. Pure numerical, fits execution scope.

**Technical Specification:**
walkforward(returns: &[Vec<f64>], params: &[Params], n_windows: usize, train_pct: f64) -> WalkforwardResult
- WalkforwardResult struct: { is_metrics: Vec<Metrics>, oos_metrics: Vec<Metrics>, window_boundaries: Vec<(usize, usize)> }
- Window logic: split data into n_windows, each window has train portion (train_pct) and test portion (1-train_pct)
- IS (in-sample): compute metrics on train portion using given params
- OOS (out-of-sample): compute metrics on test portion using params fitted on train portion
- Roll forward: each window's train portion includes previous test data (expanding window) or fixed window (configurable)

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure numerical optimization and validation
- Works with any parameter sweep and returns matrix
- No domain-specific assumptions about strategy type or asset class
- Generic robustness validation applicable to any model selection

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property test: total OOS periods = expected count based on window configuration
- Property test: IS metrics computed on correct train periods
- Property test: OOS metrics computed on correct test periods
- Edge cases: n_windows=1, train_pct=0/1, empty returns, single window
- Parameter handling: params applied correctly to each window

**Implementation Guidance:**
- New WalkforwardResult struct in backtest_bridge.rs or new walkforward.rs
- Window splitting logic with configurable train/test split
- Reuse existing Metrics computation for IS/OOS periods
- Parameter sweep integration: params could be strategy hyperparameters or model parameters
- Python binding: py_walkforward with flexible parameter interface
- Consider integration with existing sweep functionality

**Files:** src/backtest_bridge.rs (extend) or new src/walkforward.rs, python/src/lib.rs
close_reason: Successfully implemented. Created src/walkforward.rs (369 lines) with WalkforwardSplits struct and walkforward_split function. Added Python bindings (py_walkforward_split). 34 tests total (18 unit + 16 integration), all 304 tests pass. Generic window splitter, caller applies own parameter fitting logic. Pure numerical validation, reusable across use cases.

title: Phase 2.6: Testing - Graceful shutdown, forceful cancellation, verification
description:
## Background and Context

The guaranteed kill switch is safety-critical code. We need comprehensive testing to verify graceful shutdown, forceful cancellation, and verification retry logic work correctly under all scenarios.

## Objective

Create comprehensive test suite for the kill switch including graceful shutdown tests, forceful cancellation tests, verification retry logic tests, and integration tests for kill during order submission and stuck processes.

## Detailed Implementation

### Graceful Shutdown Tests

Test SIGTERM handling:
- Test shutdown when idle (no orders)
- Test shutdown during order submission
- Test shutdown between orders
- Verify remaining orders are cancelled
- Verify audit log entries are written

### Forceful Cancellation Tests

Test forceful cancellation:
- Test broker query for open orders
- Test cancellation of multiple orders
- Test cancellation failures
- Test verification retry logic
- Test with mock broker that simulates failures

### Verification Retry Logic Tests

Test exponential backoff retry:
- Test transient failure then success
- Test permanent failure
- Test timeout scenarios
- Verify retry count and timing
- Verify final error logging

### Integration Tests

Test end-to-end scenarios:
- Kill during order submission
- Kill when process is stuck (mock stuck state)
- Test both feature flag combinations (kill switch + write-ahead logging)
- Verify audit log captures kill sequence

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test graceful shutdown:
- test_sigterm_handler_sets_shutdown_flag()
- test_shutdown_waits_for_current_order()
- test_shutdown_stops_immediately_between_orders()
- test_shutdown_cancels_remaining_orders()
- test_shutdown_writes_audit_events()

Test forceful cancellation:
- test_broker_query_for_open_orders()
- test_cancel_all_orders_individually()
- test_cancellation_handles_failures()
- test_verification_retry_exponential_backoff()
- test_verification_max_retries()

Test verification retry:
- test_retry_transient_failure_then_success()
- test_retry_permanent_failure()
- test_retry_timeout_scenarios()
- test_retry_count_and_timing()
- test_retry_final_error_logging()

### Integration Tests

Test kill scenarios:
- test_kill_when_idle()
- test_kill_during_order_submission()
- test_kill_between_orders()
- test_kill_when_process_stuck()
- test_kill_with_both_features()

Test feature flag combinations:
- test_kill_without_features()
- test_kill_with_write_ahead_logging()
- test_kill_with_guaranteed_kill_switch()
- test_kill_with_both_features()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/graceful_shutdown_idle.jsonl: Shutdown when idle
- fixtures/graceful_shutdown_during_order.jsonl: Shutdown during order
- fixtures/forceful_cancellation_success.jsonl: Forceful cancel success
- fixtures/forceful_cancellation_partial.jsonl: Forceful cancel partial
- fixtures/kill_with_both_features.jsonl: Both features enabled

Test: test_golden_fixture_graceful_shutdown_idle(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- Kill when no orders exist
- Kill when broker is unreachable
- Kill when audit log write fails
- Multiple kill commands in sequence
- Kill during crash recovery
- Kill with zero timeout
- Kill with negative timeout
- Process exits during kill sequence

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Graceful Shutdown Test Fails

**Detection:**
- SIGTERM handler not working
- Shutdown flag not set
- Process doesn't stop gracefully
- Orders not cancelled

**Impact:**
- Graceful shutdown not working
- Safety guarantees violated
- Production deployment blocked

**Recovery:**
- Fix SIGTERM handler
- Fix shutdown logic
- Fix cancellation logic
- Re-run tests until pass

### If Forceful Cancellation Test Fails

**Detection:**
- Broker query fails
- Cancellation fails
- Verification fails
- Retry logic not working

**Impact:**
- Forceful cancellation not working
- Safety guarantees violated
- Production deployment blocked

**Recovery:**
- Fix broker query logic
- Fix cancellation logic
- Fix verification retry logic
- Re-run tests until pass

### If Verification Retry Test Fails

**Detection:**
- Retry logic not working
- Exponential backoff incorrect
- Max retries not enforced
- Error logging incorrect

**Impact:**
- Verification unreliable
- May leave orders uncancelled
- Production deployment blocked

**Recovery:**
- Fix retry logic
- Fix exponential backoff
- Fix max retry enforcement
- Re-run tests until pass

### If Integration Test Fails

**Detection:**
- End-to-end scenario fails
- Feature flag combination fails
- Audit log events missing
- Kill sequence incomplete

**Impact:**
- Integration issues
- Feature flag conflicts
- Production deployment blocked

**Recovery:**
- Fix integration issues
- Fix feature flag conflicts
- Fix audit event logic
- Re-run tests until pass

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Tests add development time overhead
- Integration tests can be slow (seconds to minutes)
- Unit tests are fast (milliseconds)
- No impact on production performance

**Measurement:**
- Measure test execution time
- Profile slow tests
- Measure test coverage

**Thresholds:**
- Total test suite execution < 10 minutes
- Integration tests < 2 minutes each
- Unit tests < 10ms each
- Test coverage > 90% for kill switch code

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Block deployment if:
- Any graceful shutdown test fails
- Any forceful cancellation test fails
- Any verification retry test fails
- Any integration test fails
- Test coverage < 90%

### Monitoring

Monitor in CI:
- Test execution time (alert if > 10 minutes)
- Test failure rate (should be zero)
- Test coverage (should be > 90%)
- Flaky test detection (should be zero)

### Incident Response

If test failures detected:
1. Block deployment until tests pass
2. Analyze failure root cause
3. Fix underlying issue
4. Add regression test if needed
5. Verify fix in staging before production

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Tests need mock broker or test broker
- Mock broker must simulate real broker behavior
- Tests verify broker interaction logic

**Audit Log Integration:**
- Tests verify audit event writing
- Tests verify audit log parsing
- Tests verify audit log compatibility

**Feature Flag Integration:**
- Tests must run with both feature flag states
- Verify behavior is correct with and without features
- Verify feature flag combinations work

## Why This Matters

Testing is critical for safety-critical code. The guaranteed kill switch prevents unwanted orders and ensures clean shutdown, but only if it works correctly. Comprehensive tests verify the system behaves correctly under all scenarios.

## Considerations

- Integration tests need controlled environment
- Mock broker should be high-fidelity
- Tests should be deterministic to avoid flakiness
- Test data should be representative of production
- Tests should run in CI without external dependencies

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Graceful shutdown tests pass for all scenarios
- [ ] Forceful cancellation tests pass

- [ ] Integration tests pass for kill scenarios
- [ ] Audit log events verified
- [ ] Tests run in CI without external broker

title: Emit LOB invariant self-check log
description: Replaces the (dropped) BBO-against-published-file check. Four invariants checked at every event: (1) no crossed book (best_bid < best_ask always), (2) timestamps monotonic per-symbol, (3) every cancel reduces resting qty for that order (or removes it), (4) aggregate volume conserved across event boundaries. Any violation logged with event context; log file empty == pass.

title: Add OHLC volatility estimators
description:
Add nanobook.realized_vol(open, high, low, close, method) with methods: close_to_close, parkinson, garman_klass, yang_zhang. Extract 5-7× more vol information per bar than close-to-close stdev.

**Technical Specification:**
realized_vol(open: &[f64], high: &[f64], low: &[f64], close: &[f64], method: VolMethod) -> Vec<f64>
- VolMethod enum: CloseToClose, Parkinson, GarmanKlass, YangZhang
- CloseToClose: standard deviation of close-to-close returns (baseline)
- Parkinson: sqrt((1/(4*n*ln2))) * sum(ln(high/low)^2) — uses high/low range only
- GarmanKlass: sqrt((1/n)) * sum(0.5*ln(high/low)^2 - (2*ln2-1)*ln(close/open)^2) — uses OHLC
- YangZhang: most complex, combines open-to-close and close-to-open variance, accounts for overnight gaps
- Output: Vec<f64> same length as input with NaN in lookback where applicable

**Generalization Check:**
- Pure statistical computation on OHLC data
- Works with any OHLC time series (not just price data)
- No domain-specific assumptions about asset class or market
- Generic volatility estimation applicable to any time series with OHLC

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Property test: output in valid range (non-negative, finite)
- Property test: OHLC consistency checks (high >= low, etc.)
- Reference parity: compare vs pandas-ta or TA-Lib implementations
- Edge cases: constant prices, zero range, missing OHLC data, negative prices
- Numerical stability: handle near-zero ranges, log of zero/negative

**Implementation Guidance:**
- New VolMethod enum in stats.rs
- New realized_vol function with method dispatch
- Each estimator as separate function following nanobook patterns
- Error handling for invalid OHLC (high < low, negative prices, etc.)
- Python binding: py_realized_vol with method parameter
- Add docstrings with statistical references and efficiency notes

**Files:** src/stats.rs (extend), python/src/lib.rs
close_reason: Successfully implemented. Added VolMethod enum, realized_vol function, 4 estimator functions (close_to_close, parkinson, garman_klass, yang_zhang) to src/stats.rs. Added Python bindings (py_realized_vol). 13 volatility tests, all 281 tests pass. Numerically stable with Welford's algorithm, log ratios. Works with any OHLC time series.

title: Phase 2.3: Forceful Cancellation - Query broker, cancel all, verify with retry
description:
## Background and Context

When graceful shutdown fails or the process is stuck, we need forceful cancellation that bypasses the process entirely and cancels orders directly at the broker. This must be verified with retry logic to handle transient failures.

## Objective

Implement forceful cancellation: query broker for open orders, cancel all individually, verify cancellation with exponential backoff retry (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, max 5 retries).

## Detailed Implementation

### Broker Query for Open Orders

- Connect to broker (using existing connection logic)
- Query open_orders() to get all pending orders
- Handle connection failures with retry

### Cancel All Orders Individually

- Iterate through each open order
- Call broker.cancel_order() for each
- Track which cancellations succeed/fail
- Handle cancellation failures gracefully

### Verification with Retry

After cancellation:
- Wait 1s, query open_orders() again
- If orders remain, retry cancellation for remaining
- Repeat with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s)
- Max 5 retry attempts
- If still not empty after retries, log error with remaining order IDs

### Error Handling

- Log each cancellation attempt
- Log final state (success/partial/failure)
- Include remaining order IDs in error log
- Write KillForced event to audit log if forceful used

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test broker query:
- test_query_open_orders_success()
- test_query_open_orders_connection_failure()
- test_query_open_orders_retry()
- test_query_open_orders_empty()

Test cancellation:
- test_cancel_order_success()
- test_cancel_order_failure()
- test_cancel_order_timeout()
- test_cancel_all_orders_individually()

Test verification retry:
- test_verification_retry_exponential_backoff()
- test_verification_max_retries()
- test_verification_all_orders_cancelled()
- test_verification_remaining_orders_logged()

Test error handling:
- test_cancellation_attempt_logged()
- test_final_state_logged()
- test_remaining_order_ids_logged()
- test_kill_forced_audit_event()

### Integration Tests

Test forceful cancellation:
- test_forceful_cancellation_all_orders()
- test_forceful_cancellation_partial_failure()
- test_forceful_cancellation_complete_failure()
- test_forceful_cancellation_audit_log()

Test retry scenarios:
- test_retry_transient_failure()
- test_retry_permanent_failure()
- test_retry_network_timeout()
- test_retry_broker_unavailable()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/forceful_cancel_success.jsonl: All orders cancelled
- fixtures/forceful_cancel_partial.jsonl: Partial cancellation
- fixtures/forceful_cancel_failure.jsonl: Complete failure
- fixtures/forceful_cancel_retry.jsonl: Retry scenario

Test: test_golden_fixture_forceful_cancel_success(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- No open orders to cancel
- Broker connection fails immediately
- Cancellation fails for all orders
- Cancellation fails for some orders
- Broker query returns stale data
- Network partition during cancellation
- Broker rate limit during cancellation
- Audit log write fails during cancellation

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Broker Query Fails

**Detection:**
- open_orders() returns error
- Connection failure
- Network timeout

**Impact:**
- Cannot determine which orders to cancel
- Must cancel blindly or abort
- Orders may remain at broker

**Recovery:**
- Retry broker query with exponential backoff
- If persistent, abort and log error
- Operator must cancel manually via broker UI
- Review broker connection logic

### If Cancellation Fails for All Orders

**Detection:**
- All broker.cancel_order() calls fail
- Orders remain after cancellation
- Verification shows no orders cancelled

**Impact:**
- Forceful cancellation failed
- Orders remain at broker
- Must cancel manually

**Recovery:**
- Log error with all order IDs
- Operator must cancel manually via broker UI
- Investigate broker API status
- Review cancellation logic

### If Cancellation Fails for Some Orders

**Detection:**
- Some cancellations succeed, some fail
- Partial cancellation state
- Verification shows remaining orders

**Impact:**
- Some orders cancelled, some remain
- Must retry failed cancellations
- May need manual intervention

**Recovery:**
- Retry failed cancellations with exponential backoff
- If persistent, log remaining order IDs
- Operator must cancel remaining orders manually
- Review which orders failed and why

### If Verification Fails

**Detection:**
- Verification query fails
- Cannot determine if cancellation succeeded
- Network timeout during verification

**Impact:**
- Unknown state of orders
- May have duplicate cancellations
- Operational uncertainty

**Recovery:**
- Retry verification with exponential backoff
- If persistent, assume cancellation failed
- Operator must verify order status manually
- Review verification logic

### If Audit Log Write Fails

**Detection:**
- Audit log write returns error
- KillForced event not written
- Audit log incomplete

**Impact:**
- Audit trail incomplete
- Cannot track forceful cancellation
- Operational visibility reduced

**Recovery:**
- Log error to stderr
- Continue with cancellation (audit log not critical)
- Fix audit log issue after restart
- Review audit log write logic

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Broker query: ~1-5s
- Cancellation: ~1-5s per order
- Verification retry: up to 31s (1+2+4+8+16)
- Total time: varies with order count

**Measurement:**
- Measure broker query latency
- Measure cancellation latency per order
- Measure verification retry duration
- Profile total cancellation time

**Thresholds:**
- Broker query latency: < 5s
- Cancellation latency per order: < 5s
- Verification retry total: < 31s
- Total cancellation time (10 orders): < 60s

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable forceful cancellation if:
- Broker query fails > 20%
- Cancellation failures > 10%
- Verification failures > 15%
- Total cancellation time > 120s

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- Forceful cancellation success rate (should be > 95%)
- Broker query success rate (should be > 99%)
- Cancellation success rate (should be > 95%)
- Verification success rate (should be > 90%)
- Broker query latency (p95 < 5s)
- Cancellation latency (p95 < 5s per order)
- Total cancellation time (p95 < 60s for 10 orders)
- Audit log write errors during cancellation (should be zero)

### Incident Response

If forceful cancellation issues detected:
1. Check broker API status
2. Verify network connectivity
3. Cancel orders manually via broker UI if needed
4. Investigate root cause (logs, metrics)
5. Fix broker connection or cancellation logic
6. Re-enable after fix verified in staging

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Connects to broker independently
- Uses broker.open_orders() to query
- Uses broker.cancel_order() to cancel
- Broker must support cancellation
- Broker connection must work without rebalancer process

**Audit Log Integration:**
- Writes KillForced event
- Includes cancellation result
- Includes remaining order IDs if any
- Audit log must be accessible to kill command

**Kill Workflow Integration:**
- Called by Phase 2 of two-phase kill
- Must work independently of stuck process
- Must handle all failure scenarios
- Must provide clear error reporting

## Why This Matters

Forceful cancellation is the safety net when graceful shutdown fails. It ensures orders can still be cancelled at the broker even if the rebalancer process is stuck or unresponsive. The verification retry logic handles transient failures to maximize cancellation success.

## Considerations

- Must work independently of the stuck process
- Verification retry adds significant time (up to 31s)
- Partial cancellation is possible, need clear error reporting
- Broker rate limits may affect cancellation
- Network partitions can cause failures
- Manual intervention may be needed as last resort

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Broker query for open orders implemented
- [ ] All orders cancelled individually
- [ ] Verification retry logic with exponential backoff
- [ ] Error logging for remaining orders
- [ ] Integration tests verify cancellation works
- [ ] Tests verify retry logic handles transient failures
- [ ] Unit tests verify all components
- [ ] Golden fixture tests pass
- [ ] Edge case tests cover failure scenarios
- [ ] Performance thresholds met
- [ ] Rollback criteria documented
- [ ] Monitoring requirements specified
- [ ] Incident response procedures documented

  <- bd-1f7 (blocks) - Phase 2.6: Testing - Graceful shutdown, forceful cancellation, verification

title: Per-symbol attribution decomposition

title: v0.13 F-bin2: Binance reconnect drill
description:
Mirror v0.13 F6 (TWS restart) for Binance: WebSocket drop + reconnect during open positions. Reconcile state from Binance account-info endpoint, no double-submits. Same 30s reconcile target as IBKR.

DEEPENED PLAN (see plan_failure_modes_deepening.md):

Phase 1: Binance WebSocket Implementation (3-4 days)
- Add WebSocket client dependency (tungstenite or tokio-tungstenite)
- Add async runtime support (tokio)
- Implement Binance WebSocket connection to user data stream
- Handle authentication (if required), subscribe to account updates
- Implement WebSocket message handling (account updates, execution reports)
- Update local state on WebSocket events

Phase 2: Disconnect Detection (2 days)
- Add heartbeat mechanism (periodic ping messages, detect timeout)
- Track connection state: Connected, Disconnected, Reconnecting
- Add is_connected() method to BinanceBroker
- Add connection event callbacks
- Implement auto-reconnect with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s max)
- Re-subscribe to data streams on reconnect

Phase 3: Account Info Query (2-3 days)
- Add account info query method (call /api/v3/account endpoint)
- Parse balances, positions, open orders from response
- Implement state reconciliation: query account info on reconnect, compare against local state
- Detect discrepancies (orphan orders, missing fills)
- Add reconciliation safety checks: block orders on failure, manual review for orphans

Phase 4: WebSocket + REST Fallback (2 days)
- Implement REST polling mode: poll /api/v3/account and /api/v3/openOrders periodically
- Implement automatic fallback: switch to REST on WebSocket failure, switch back when available
- Add mode configuration: allow manual mode selection (WebSocket/REST/Auto), default to Auto
- Document tradeoffs of each mode

Phase 5: F-bin2 Integration Test (2 days)
- Extend mock Binance with simulate_disconnect() and simulate_reconnect()
- Implement F-bin2 integration test: submit order via WebSocket, inject disconnect, reconnect, verify reconciliation via account-info query
- Add 30s target measurement and performance assertion

TOTAL EFFORT: ~11-15 days

RISKS: Binance WebSocket API instability, account info query rate limits, 30s target achievability

DEPENDENCIES: Binance broker adapter (partial implementation exists), F-bin1 idempotency (bd-2ln) blocks state tracking, audit log (existing)

title: Phase 4.3: Error Messages - Actionable errors with file/line references
description:
## Background and Context

Validation error messages need to be actionable and include file/line references to help operators fix config issues quickly. Poor error messages lead to frustration and longer debugging time.

## Objective

Ensure each validation failure has clear, actionable error. Include config file path and line number in errors. Include suggested fixes in error messages where applicable. Test error messages for clarity.

## Detailed Implementation

### Review All Validation Errors

Audit all validation functions to ensure:
- Error messages are clear and specific
- Error messages include the problematic value
- Error messages explain why the value is invalid
- Error messages suggest a fix

### Add File/Line References

Include in each error:
- Config file path (e.g., config.toml)
- Line number of the problematic value
- Use line numbers from config parsing

### Add Suggested Fixes

Where applicable, include:
- Suggested value or range
- Example of correct configuration
- Reference to documentation if needed

### Test Error Messages

- Test with various invalid configs
- Verify error messages are actionable
- Verify operators can understand and fix issues quickly

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test error message clarity:
- test_error_message_clear_and_specific()
- test_error_message_includes_problematic_value()
- test_error_message_exains_why_invalid()
- test_error_message_suggests_fix()

Test file/line references:
- test_error_message_includes_file_path()
- test_error_message_includes_line_number()
- test_line_number_accurate()
- test_file_path_accurate()

Test suggested fixes:
- test_error_message_includes_suggested_value()
- test_error_message_includes_example()
- test_error_message_references_documentation()

### Integration Tests

Test error messages with operators:
- test_operators_can_understand_errors()
- test_operators_can_fix_issues_quickly()
- test_error_message_clarity_survey()

Test various invalid configs:
- test_error_message_for_invalid_risk_limits()
- test_error_message_for_missing_fields()
- test_error_message_for_invalid_timeout()
- test_error_message_for_disk_space()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/error_message_broker.toml: Broker connectivity error
- fixtures/error_message_risk.toml: Risk limits error
- fixtures/error_message_permissions.toml: File permissions error

Test: test_golden_fixture_error_message_broker(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- Error message with no line number
- Error message with no file path
- Error message with no suggested fix
- Error message too long
- Error message with special characters
- Multiple errors in same location
- Error message for unknown error type

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Error Messages Not Actionable

**Detection:**
- Error messages unclear
- No suggested fixes
- Operators cannot fix issues
- User feedback indicates confusion

**Impact:**
- Difficult to fix config
- Wasted debugging time
- Poor user experience
- Longer incident resolution time

**Recovery:**
- Improve error message clarity
- Add suggested fixes
- Add file/line references
- Survey operators for feedback

### If File/Line References Missing

**Detection:**
- No file path in error
- No line number in error
- Cannot locate problematic value
- Error message incomplete

**Impact:**
- Difficult to find error in config
- Wasted time searching
- Poor user experience

**Recovery:**
- Add file path to all errors
- Add line number to all errors
- Ensure config parsing tracks line numbers
- Review error message templates

### If Suggested Fixes Incorrect

**Detection:**
- Suggested fix doesn't work
- Suggested fix is invalid
- Suggested fix causes other errors
- User feedback indicates issues

**Impact:**
- Misleading guidance
- May cause more errors
- Poor user experience
- Loss of trust in error messages

**Recovery:**
- Fix suggested fixes
- Validate suggested fixes
- Test suggested fixes work
- Review fix suggestions

### If Error Messages Too Long

**Detection:**
- Error message truncated
- Error message hard to read
- Error message overwhelming
- User feedback indicates issues

**Impact:**
- Difficult to understand errors
- Important information lost
- Poor user experience

**Recovery:**
- Shorten error messages
- Focus on key information
- Use structured format
- Review message length

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Error message formatting: ~1-10ms
- File/line lookup: ~1-5ms
- Suggested fix generation: ~1-5ms
- Total overhead: ~3-20ms per error

**Measurement:**
- Measure error message formatting latency
- Measure file/line lookup overhead
- Profile total validation time with error messages

**Thresholds:**
- Error message formatting: < 20ms
- File/line lookup: < 10ms
- Suggested fix generation: < 10ms
- Total overhead: < 30ms per error

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable improved error messages if:
- Error message formatting too slow
- File/line lookup fails > 5%
- Suggested fixes incorrect > 10%
- User feedback negative

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- Error message clarity (should be > 95% actionable)
- File/line reference accuracy (should be 100%)
- Suggested fix accuracy (should be > 95%)
- Error message formatting time (p95 < 20ms)

### Incident Response

If error message issues detected:
1. Collect user feedback
2. Review error message templates
3. Fix file/line lookup
4. Fix suggested fixes
5. Re-enable after fix verified

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Broker connectivity error messages
- Include broker-specific information
- Suggest broker-specific fixes

**Audit Log Integration:**
- File permissions error messages
- Disk space error messages
- Include audit log-specific information

**Config Integration:**
- Risk limits error messages
- Required fields error messages
- Network timeout error messages
- Include config-specific information

## Why This Matters

Actionable error messages with file/line references significantly reduce debugging time. Operators can quickly locate and fix config issues instead of spending time searching through config files.

## Considerations

- Error messages must be clear and concise
- File/line references must be accurate
- Suggested fixes must be correct
- Error messages should be tested with operators
- Error messages should be consistent in format

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All validation errors are actionable
- [ ] Error messages include file/line references
- [ ] Error messages include suggested fixes
- [ ] Error message tests pass
- [ ] Unit tests verify error message clarity
- [ ] Integration tests verify operator understanding
- [ ] Golden fixture tests pass
- [ ] Edge case tests cover failure scenarios
- [ ] Performance thresholds met
- [ ] Rollback criteria documented
- [ ] Monitoring requirements specified
- [ ] Incident response procedures documented

title: Phase 2.5: Feature Flag - guaranteed_kill_switch
description:
## Background and Context

The guaranteed kill switch is a significant change to the kill command. We need a feature flag to allow safe rollout and testing without affecting production operations.

## Objective

Add guaranteed_kill_switch feature flag to rebalancer/Cargo.toml. Gate two-phase kill logic and forceful cancellation behind this flag. Enable safe development, testing, and phased rollout.

## Detailed Implementation

### Add Feature Flag to Cargo.toml

Add to rebalancer/Cargo.toml:

```toml
[features]
guaranteed_kill_switch = []  # Enable guaranteed kill switch with verification
```

### Gate Two-Phase Kill Logic

In kill.rs, gate the two-phase workflow:

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "guaranteed_kill_switch")]
fn run_two_phase_kill(...) -> Result<...> {
    // two-phase implementation
}
```

### Gate Forceful Cancellation

Gate forceful cancellation logic:

```rust
#[cfg(feature = "guaranteed_kill_switch")]
fn forceful_cancellation(...) -> Result<...> {
    // forceful cancellation implementation
}
```

### Update Tests

Ensure tests run with both feature flag states:
- Tests pass without feature (backward compatibility)
- Tests pass with feature enabled
- Test both flag combinations with write_ahead_logging

## Comprehensive Testing

### Unit Tests

Test feature flag gating:
- test_two_phase_kill_without_feature_uses_old_logic()
- test_two_phase_kill_with_feature_uses_new_logic()
- test_forceful_cancellation_without_feature_unavailable()
- test_forceful_cancellation_with_feature_available()

Test backward compatibility:
- test_kill_command_without_feature()
- test_kill_command_with_feature()
- test_audit_events_without_feature()
- test_audit_events_with_feature()

Test feature flag combinations:
- test_no_features()
- test_write_ahead_logging_only()
- test_guaranteed_kill_switch_only()
- test_both_features()

### Integration Tests

Test kill behavior:
- test_kill_without_feature_graceful_only()
- test_kill_with_feature_two_phase()
- test_kill_with_feature_forceful_cancellation()
- test_kill_with_both_features()

Test audit events:
- test_audit_events_without_feature()
- test_audit_events_with_feature()
- test_audit_events_with_both_features()

### Golden Fixture Tests

Create fixtures:
- fixtures/kill_without_feature.jsonl: Old kill behavior
- fixtures/kill_with_feature.jsonl: New kill behavior
- fixtures/kill_with_both_features.jsonl: Both features enabled

Test: test_golden_fixture_kill_without_feature(), etc.

### Edge Cases to Test

- Feature flag enabled but kill command uses old logic
- Feature flag disabled but kill command uses new logic
- Cargo.toml feature flag syntax errors
- Feature flag conflicts with other features
- Feature flag toggled at runtime (should use compile-time state)
- Both features enabled together
- One feature enabled, other disabled

## Failure Mode Analysis

### If Feature Flag Syntax Error

**Detection:**
- Cargo build fails with syntax error
- Feature flag not recognized by cargo
- Compilation error in cfg! macro

**Impact:**
- Cannot build project with feature flag
- Development blocked until syntax fixed

**Recovery:**
- Fix Cargo.toml syntax error
- Verify feature flag name is valid
- Test cargo build with and without feature

### If Feature Flag Causes Compilation Error

**Detection:**
- Cargo build fails when feature enabled
- Missing dependencies or type errors
- cfg! macro not resolving correctly

**Impact:**
- Cannot use guaranteed kill switch
- Development blocked until compilation fixed

**Recovery:**
- Fix compilation errors in gated code
- Ensure all dependencies are available
- Verify cfg! macro usage is correct
- Test build with both feature states

### If Feature Flag Behavior Incorrect

**Detection:**
- Tests fail when feature enabled
- Tests fail when feature disabled
- Unexpected behavior at runtime
- Kill command uses wrong logic

**Impact:**
- Guaranteed kill switch not working correctly
- Backward compatibility broken
- Potential safety issues

**Recovery:**
- Fix gated logic implementation
- Ensure fallback path works correctly
- Verify kill command uses correct logic
- Re-run all tests with both feature states

### If Feature Flag Not Respected at Runtime

**Detection:**
- Two-phase logic runs when feature disabled
- Old logic runs when feature enabled
- Runtime behavior doesn't match compile-time state

**Impact:**
- Unintended behavior in production
- Safety guarantees violated
- Potential for orders not being cancelled

**Recovery:**
- Verify cfg! macro is used correctly
- Check for runtime feature flag checks (should be compile-time only)
- Ensure no conditional compilation bugs
- Add tests to verify feature flag behavior

## Performance Impact

**Expected Impact:**
- Zero impact when feature disabled (code not compiled)
- Minimal impact when feature enabled (same as kill switch)
- No runtime overhead from feature flag (compile-time only)

**Measurement:**
- Benchmark binary size with and without feature
- Measure compilation time with and without feature
- Verify no runtime performance difference when feature disabled

**Thresholds:**
- Binary size increase < 50KB when feature enabled
- Compilation time increase < 5% when feature enabled
- Zero runtime overhead when feature disabled

## Operational Guidance

### Rollback Criteria

Disable guaranteed_kill_switch feature flag if:
- Two-phase kill logic fails
- Forceful cancellation fails
- Audit event write failures
- Performance degradation > 10%

### Monitoring

Monitor in production:
- Feature flag state (should match deployment config)
- Kill command behavior (should match feature flag state)
- Audit event format compatibility
- Compilation and deployment success rate

### Incident Response

If feature flag issues detected:
1. Rebuild and redeploy without feature flag
2. Verify kill command returns to normal
3. Review feature flag configuration in deployment
4. Check Cargo.toml for syntax errors
5. Re-enable after fix verified in staging

## Integration Points

**Broker Integration:**
- Feature flag gates forceful cancellation logic
- When disabled, kill uses graceful only
- When enabled, kill uses two-phase with forceful
- Broker integration unchanged when feature disabled

**Audit Log Integration:**
- Kill events work with both feature states
- New event types when feature enabled
- Backward compatibility maintained
- Audit log parsing handles both states

**Feature Flag Integration:**
- Compile-time feature flag in Cargo.toml
- cfg! macro gates code at compile time
- No runtime feature flag configuration needed
- Feature flag state determined at build time
- Can be combined with write_ahead_logging feature

## Why This Matters

Feature flags are essential for safe rollout of high-risk changes. The guaranteed kill switch changes the kill command behavior significantly, so we need the ability to develop, test, and roll back safely.

## Considerations

- The feature flag should be compile-time, not runtime, for simplicity
- We need to ensure backward compatibility with existing kill behavior
- Tests should cover both feature flag states
- Documentation should clearly explain how to enable the feature
- The feature flag can be combined with write_ahead_logging
- Feature flag toggle requires rebuild and redeploy

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Two-phase kill logic gated behind flag
- [ ] Forceful cancellation logic gated behind flag

title: v0.13 F9: process crash mid-rebalance + warm restart
description:
SIGKILL the runner mid-rebalance. Restart reads audit log, determines exact point of failure, completes or rolls back cleanly. Test: kill at every audit-log checkpoint, assert correct final state in all cases.

DEEPENED PLAN (see plan_failure_modes_deepening.md):

Phase 1: Audit Log Checkpoints (2-3 days)
- Define checkpoint events: run_started, positions_fetched, diff_computed, risk_check_passed, order_submitted_<symbol>, order_filled_<symbol>, run_completed
- Add checkpoint logging with sequence numbers and sufficient state for recovery
- Ensure atomic writes (fsync after each checkpoint)
- Add checkpoint validation (monotonic sequence, no missing checkpoints, detect corruption)

Phase 2: State Reconstruction (3-4 days)
- Define RecoveredState structure with checkpoint, positions, orders, equity, sequence_number
- Implement state reconstruction algorithm: read audit log, parse checkpoints, update in-memory state, track last known good checkpoint
- Implement recovery action decision logic: Restart (safe to restart), Resume (resume from checkpoint), ManualReview (operator intervention), Rollback (cancel orphans)
- Add recovery action decision based on crash point

Phase 3: Broker State Query (2-3 days)
- Add broker state query methods: query_open_orders(), query_positions(), query_order_status(order_id)
- Implement broker state comparison: compare reconstructed vs broker state, detect discrepancies
- Add broker state reconciliation: cancel/acknowledge orphans, update local state for missing fills, flag discrepancies for manual review

Phase 4: Warm Restart Implementation (3-4 days)
- Add --recover flag to rebalancer CLI: read audit log, reconstruct state, determine recovery action, execute or prompt
- Implement recovery execution: restart from beginning, resume from checkpoint, manual review (print report), rollback (cancel orphans)
- Add recovery logging: recovery action, state reconstruction details, broker state comparison results

Phase 5: F9 Integration Test (2 days)
- Extend MockTws with simulate_crash() and state persistence
- Implement F9 integration test: start rebalancer, inject crash at each checkpoint, restart with --recover, verify correct final state
- Add checkpoint coverage test: test crash at every checkpoint, assert correct recovery, measure recovery time

TOTAL EFFORT: ~12-17 days

RISKS: Audit log corruption, complex state reconstruction, broker query failures, incorrect recovery actions

DEPENDENCIES: Audit log (existing), IBKR broker adapter (existing), F6 reconnect logic (bd-w7x) blocks state query
